content: Marie Curie and her husband Pierre worked for years in a leaking shed, processing tonnes of pitchblende ore by hand. They discovered that the ore emitted far more radiation than could be explained by uranium alone. After years of painstaking work, they isolated two new elements: polonium (named after Marie's homeland Poland) and radium.

Marie Curie was the first woman to win a Nobel Prize and remains the only person to win Nobel Prizes in two different sciences. She shared the 1903 Nobel Prize in Physics with Pierre Curie and Henri Becquerel for their work on radioactivity. In 1911, she won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry for the discovery of polonium and radium.

In Marie's time, women were not permitted to attend university in Poland. She secretly attended the 'Flying University', illegal underground classes. She saved money for years to attend the Sorbonne in Paris. When she won her first Nobel Prize, some tried to give all credit to Pierre. She became the first female professor at the Sorbonne in 1906.

Radium is a highly radioactive element that glows a faint blue-green in the dark. Marie Curie kept samples in her desk drawer and carried test tubes in her pockets, not knowing the danger. Radium's radioactivity is 2.7 million times stronger than uranium. It was initially used in luminous paint for watch dials and in early cancer treatments.

During World War I, Marie Curie developed mobile X-ray units called 'petites Curies' that could be driven to the front lines to help surgeons locate bullets and shrapnel in wounded soldiers. She trained women to operate these units. This practical application of her scientific work saved countless lives during the war.

Marie Curie died on July 4, 1934, from aplastic anemia, almost certainly caused by years of radiation exposure. Her notebooks are still so radioactive that they must be stored in lead-lined boxes, and visitors must sign a liability waiver to access them. Despite the personal cost, her work revolutionized medicine and our understanding of atomic structure.

Marie Curie opened doors for women in science at a time when few could even attend university. She said: 'Nothing in life is to be feared, it is only to be understood.' Her daughter Irène Joliot-Curie also won a Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1935, making the Curies the family with the most Nobel Prize winners.

Polonium was the first element Marie Curie discovered, in 1898. She named it after Poland, which at the time did not exist as an independent country — it was divided between Russia, Prussia, and Austria. Naming the element was a political statement supporting Polish independence. Polonium is extremely rare and highly radioactive.
